content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 25 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 112r | **Source Images:** DEU_025_page_001.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 158 | **Images:** DEU_025_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** "When men have a dispute and come to court, and the judges decide between them, they shall acquit the innocent and condemn the guilty.

**2** And if the guilty man deserves to be beaten, the judge shall cause him to lie down and be beaten in his presence with a number of stripes in proportion to his offense.

**3** Forty stripes may be given him, but not more, lest, if more stripes were added than these, your brother be degraded in your sight.

**4** You shall not muzzle an ox when it is treading out the grain.

**5** "If brothers dwell together, and one of them dies and has no son, the wife of the dead man shall not be married outside the family to a stranger. Her husband's brother shall go in to her and take her as his wife and perform the duty of a husband's brother to her.

**6** And the firstborn whom she bears shall succeed in the name of his dead brother, so that his name may not be blotted out from Israel.

**7** And if the man does not wish to take his brother's wife, then his brother's wife shall go up to the gate to the elders and say, 'My husband's brother refuses to perpetuate his brother's name in Israel; he will not perform the duty of a husband's brother to me.'

**8** Then the elders of his city shall call him and speak to him, and if he persists and says, 'I do not wish to take her,'

**9** then his brother's wife shall come up to him in the presence of the elders and pull his sandal off his foot and spit in his face. And she shall answer and say, 'So shall it be done to the man who does not build up his brother's house.'

**10** And the name of his house shall be called in Israel, 'The house of the unsandaled.'

**11** "When men fight with one another and the wife of the one draws near to rescue her husband from the hand of him who is striking him, and puts out her hand and seizes him by his private parts,

**12** then you shall cut off her hand. Your eye shall have no pity.

**13** "You shall not have in your bag two kinds of weights, a large and a small.

**14** You shall not have in your house two kinds of measures, a large and a small.

**15** A full and just weight you shall have, a full and just measure you shall have, that your days may be long in the land that the LORD your God is giving you.

**16** For all who do such things, all who act dishonestly, are an abomination to the LORD your God.

**17** "Remember what Amalek did to you on the way as you came out of Egypt,

**18** how he attacked you on the way, when you were faint and weary, and cut off your tail, those who were lagging behind you, and he did not fear God.

**19** Therefore when the LORD your God has given you rest from all your enemies around you, in the land that the LORD your God is giving you for an inheritance to possess, you shall blot out the memory of Amalek from under heaven; you shall not forget."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Deuteronomy 25**

**Levirate marriage — yibbum (vv. 5–10):** The Hebrew term for the brother's duty is *yibbum* (יִבּוּם), from the root *yavam* (brother-in-law). I render "perform the duty of a husband's brother" rather than the single-word "levirate" (from Latin *levir*) in order to preserve transparency. The LXX translates the brother-in-law as *ho adelphos tou andros* (ὁ ἀδελφὸς τοῦ ἀνδρός, "the brother of the husband") and the duty as *antilempsetai auten* (ἀντιλήψεται αὐτήν, "will take her to himself"), closely following the Hebrew.

**Chalitzah / sandal ceremony (vv. 7–10):** The rite of *chalitzah* (חֲלִיצָה, "loosing") releases the man from his obligation. The pulling off of the sandal symbolizes forfeiture of his right of redemption. The LXX preserves *hypodesin* (ὑπόδεσιν, "sandal") and *emptuse* (ἐμπτύσει, "shall spit"), matching the Hebrew precisely.

**"Cut off your tail" (v. 18):** *va-yezanev bekha* — literally "and he tailed you," i.e., struck at those following behind. The image is of a predator attacking the straggling rear of a column. I render "cut off your tail" to preserve the vivid directional image. The LXX has *ekopteto sou ten ouragian* (ἐκοπτέτο σου τὴν οὐραγίαν, "cut off your rear guard"), a slightly military rendering.

**Forty stripes minus one (vv. 2–3):** The Hebrew specifies "forty" (*arba'im*) as the maximum; the practice of "forty minus one" (thirty-nine) familiar from Paul (2 Cor 11:24) was rabbinic caution to avoid accidentally exceeding forty. The text itself says forty. The LXX reads *tesserakonta* (τεσσαράκοντα, "forty"), confirming the Hebrew.

**Honest weights — even shlemah (vv. 13–15):** *even shlemah va-tsedek* — "a complete and just weight." I render "a full and just weight" to capture *shlemah* (from *shalem*, wholeness/completeness). The LXX has *stathmon alethine kai dikaion* (σταθμὸν ἀληθινὸν καὶ δίκαιον, "a true and just weight"), choosing "true" over "full" — a slight interpretive shift away from the concrete integrity of measurement toward epistemic truth.

**"Blot out the memory of Amalek" (v. 19):** *timcheh et-zekher Amaleq* — "you shall blot out/wipe out the memory/name of Amalek." The verb *machah* (מָחָה) means to wipe clean, erase. "Memory" renders *zekher* (זֵכֶר), which encompasses name, reputation, and legacy. The LXX has *exaleipseis to mnemosynon Amalek* (ἐξαλείψεις τὸ μνημόσυνον Αμαλήκ), "you shall wipe out the memorial of Amalek," closely equivalent.

**"He did not fear God" (v. 18):** *ve-lo yare Elohim* — the use of the generic *Elohim* rather than the covenant name YHWH in this characterization of Amalek is notable. The LXX has *ou phoboumenos ton Theon* (οὐ φοβούμενος τὸν Θεόν), matching the Hebrew.

— LOGOS
